option.name: fileSummaryMaps
option.value: {"C:/Users/NAVEEN/IdeaProjects/untitled":"{\"/src/basic.java\":\"This Java file defines a basic class with a main method that:\\n\\n1. Declares an integer variable x and initializes it to 1.\\n\\n2. Declares an integer array arr but does not initialize its size. \\n\\n3. Initializes the first element of arr to 1 by assigning arr[0] \\u003d 1.\\n\\n4. Initializes the second element of arr to 2 by assigning arr[1] \\u003d 2. \\n\\n5. Uses a for loop from i \\u003d 0 to i \\u003c arr.length to iterate through the array.\\n\\n6. Inside the for loop, it prints out each element of arr using System.out.println(arr[i]).\\n\\nSo in summary, the key functions are:\\n\\n- Define a class with a main method \\n- Declare integer variables\\n- Declare and initialize an array\\n- Assign values to array elements\\n- Use a for loop to iterate through the array\\n- Print out array elements\\n\\nIt demonstrates basic array initialization and iteration in Java.\",\"/src/sorting/selectionsort.java\":\"This Java file implements selection sort on an integer array.\\n\\nKey functions:\\n\\n- main() - The main method that runs the sorting algorithm. It takes an unsorted integer array as input.\\n\\n- swaap(int[], int, int) - A helper method that swaps two elements in the array. Takes the array, index of first element, and index of second element.\\n\\n- Selection sort algorithm:\\n - For each index i from 0 to length of array\\n - Find the minimum element in the remaining unsorted portion (from i to end) \\n - Swap the minimum element with the element at index i\\n\\n- To find the minimum:\\n - Initialize min to maximum possible value\\n - Iterate from i to end of array \\n - Update min and its index if a smaller element is found\\n\\n- After the inner loop, the minimum element is swapped with the element at index i.\\n\\n- This brings the minimum element to its correct sorted position in each iteration.\\n\\n- Prints the sorted array after completion of algorithm.\\n\\nSo in summary, it implements classic selection sort to sort an integer array in place.\",\"/src/OOPS/algebraclass.java\":\"This Java file defines a simple algebra class and demonstrates its usage:\\n\\n- It defines an algebra class that contains:\\n - An instance variable x initialized to 23\\n - An add() method that takes two int parameters x and y, adds them and returns the sum\\n\\n- A main() method is defined in the algebraclass class:\\n - It creates an instance of the algebra class\\n - Takes two integer inputs from the user using Scanner\\n - Calls the add() method on the algebra instance, passing the user inputs\\n - Prints the returned sum\\n\\nKey functions:\\n\\n- algebra class:\\n - Defines the algebra class\\n - Contains an instance variable x\\n - add() method that adds two integers and returns the sum\\n\\n- main() method:\\n - Creates an algebra instance \\n - Gets integer inputs from user\\n - Calls add() method, passing inputs\\n - Prints returned sum\\n\\nSo in summary, it defines a simple algebra class with an addition method, creates an instance, calls the add method to demonstrate its usage, and prints the result.\",\"/src/sorting/Insertionsort.java\":\"This Java file implements insertion sort on an integer array:\\n\\n- It contains a main method that defines an integer array arr with sample values.\\n\\n- The key function is the for loop that iterates from i\\u003d0 to the length of the array. This is the outer loop that selects each element to insert in sorted order. \\n\\n- Inside the for loop will be the insertion sort logic:\\n - It takes the current element arr[i] and compares it to the elements before it to find the correct insertion position\\n - It shifts elements over to make space for the new element\\n - It inserts arr[i] into the correct position\\n\\n- So the key functions are:\\n - The outer for loop that selects each element \\n - The insertion sort logic inside the loop to shift and insert each element\\n - Comparing elements to find the correct insertion point\\n - Shifting elements over to make space\\n - Inserting the element into the sorted position\\n\\n- This implements a basic insertion sort on the integer array to sort it in ascending order.\\n\\nSo in summary, it uses a for loop and insertion sort logic to iteratively sort the elements of an integer array from lowest to highest value.\",\"/src/leetcode/meargArray.java\":\"This Java file contains code to merge two sorted arrays into a single sorted array.\\n\\nThe key functions/aspects are:\\n\\n- It defines a main method to run the code.\\n\\n- It declares and initializes two input arrays - arr and brr, with arr being larger. \\n\\n- It declares an output array ans with a length equal to the sum of the input array lengths.\\n\\n- It uses three indices j, k, i to iterate through the input and output arrays.\\n\\n- In each iteration of the main loop:\\n - It compares the current elements of the input arrays arr[j] and brr[k]\\n - Whichever element is smaller is added to the next position in ans\\n - The index of the array that element came from is incremented\\n\\n- It handles edge cases when one input array is exhausted by only incrementing the index of the other array\\n\\n- After the merge, it prints out the merged sorted array ans\\n\\nSo in summary, it takes two sorted input arrays, merges them together while maintaining sort order, and outputs the result to a third array.\",\"/src/OOPS/classAndobjects.java\":\"This Java file defines a class called students and demonstrates creating objects of that class.\\n\\nKey things it does:\\n\\n1. Defines a class called students with fields for age and name.\\n\\n2. Includes two constructors - a default constructor and a parameterized constructor that initializes the fields. \\n\\n3. In the main method, it creates an object of the students class called naveen using the default constructor. \\n\\n4. Prints out the age field of the naveen object to demonstrate accessing an instance field.\\n\\nSo in summary:\\n\\n- It defines a class with fields \\n- Includes constructors\\n- Creates an object of that class\\n- Accesses fields of the object\\n\\nThis demonstrates some basic object oriented concepts in Java like:\\n\\n- Class definition\\n- Fields \\n- Constructors\\n- Object creation\\n- Accessing object fields\\n\\nSo it provides a simple example of defining a class, creating objects, and interacting with the objects\\u0027 fields to understand basic OOP concepts in Java.\",\"/src/calculation/MINelementinArray.java\":\"This Java file finds the minimum element in an integer array and its index.\\n\\nKey functions:\\n\\n- Defines an integer array called arr with sample values {9,8,7,6,0,5,4,3,2,1}\\n- Initializes an integer variable min to the maximum possible integer value (Integer.MAX_VALUE) to start tracking the minimum\\n- Initializes an integer variable x to track the index of the minimum element \\n- Loops through the array from index 0 to length-1\\n- Compares each element to the current min value using a if statement\\n- If the element is less than min, it updates min to that element value and x to the current index\\n- After the loop, min will hold the minimum element value in the array\\n- x will hold the index of that minimum element\\n- Prints out the minimum value and its index\\n\\nSo in summary, it finds the minimum integer value in the given array and its corresponding index through iteration and comparison.\",\"/src/leetcode/threesum.java\":\"This Java file contains a program to solve the three sum problem:\\n\\nKey functions:\\n\\n- main(): The main entry point of the program.\\n\\n- Arrays.sort(arr): Sorts the input array arr in ascending order. This is needed to efficiently solve the three sum problem. \\n\\nNested for loops:\\n - Loops through each element i in arr\\n - Loops through each element j from i+1 to end of arr\\n - Loops through each element k from j+1 to end of arr\\n\\n- Checks if sum of arr[i] + arr[j] + arr[k] is 0\\n- Increments count if sum is 0\\n\\n- Creates a 2D array brr to store all triplets that sum to 0\\n- Stores each valid triplet (arr[i], arr[j], arr[k]) in brr\\n\\n- Prints the count of valid triplets \\n- Prints all triplets stored in brr\\n\\nSo in summary, it takes a sorted input array, finds all unique triplets that sum to 0 using nested loops, stores them in a 2D array and prints the output.\",\"/src/calculation/twoDarray.java\":\"This Java file defines a class called twoDarray that contains a main method to demonstrate working with 2D arrays:\\n\\nKey functions:\\n\\n- Declares a 2D integer array brr with 3 rows and 2 columns and initializes some elements\\n- Prints the element at brr[2][1] to output 1\\n\\n- Declares a 2D integer array arr initialized with values \\n- Uses a for loop to iterate through the columns (length) of the first row (arr[0])\\n- Prints the element at arr[0][0] on each iteration, which will output 2 repeatedly\\n\\nSo in summary:\\n\\n- It shows how to declare and initialize 2D arrays \\n- Access elements at specific indices\\n- Loop through a row to access all column elements\\n\\nThe purpose is to demonstrate basic operations like initialization, access, and iteration with 2D arrays in Java. The main() method acts as a simple test/example to output the results.\",\"/src/leetcode/mergeusingstack.java\":\"This Java file is merging two sorted arrays arr and brr into a single sorted array ans using a stack.\\n\\nThe key functions/steps are:\\n\\n1. It defines two input arrays - arr and brr that are already sorted. \\n\\n2. It initializes variables j, k, l to track the indexes of the input and output arrays. \\n\\n3. It creates the output array ans with a size equal to the sum of arr and brr lengths.\\n\\n4. It uses a while loop to iterate through ans and compare elements of arr and brr at each step:\\n\\n - It compares arr[j] and brr[k] and inserts the smaller element into ans[l]\\n\\n - It increments the index of the array where the element was taken from \\n\\n5. This process continues until all elements of arr and brr have been processed and inserted into ans\\n\\n6. No stack is actually used. It merges the arrays in-place without any additional data structures like stack or queue.\\n\\nSo in summary, it merges two sorted arrays into a single sorted output array by sequentially comparing and inserting elements from the input arrays into the output array.\",\"/src/sorting/bubblesort.java\":\"This Java file implements bubble sort sorting algorithm.\\n\\nKey functions:\\n\\n- swap(int[] arr, int j) - Swaps the elements at index j and j+1 in the array. This is the core swap operation used during sorting.\\n\\n- main(String[] args) - The main method:\\n\\n - Declares and initializes an integer array to sort\\n\\n - Has two nested for loops to iterate through the array\\n\\n - The outer loop iterates from 0 to length-1, to traverse the entire array\\n\\n - The inner loop iterates from 0 to length-1-i, where i is the index of outer loop. This ensures the sorted elements are not traversed again.\\n\\n - It compares adjacent elements arr[j] and arr[j+1] and swaps them if out of order using the swap function.\\n\\n - Prints the sorted array after completion\\n\\nSo in summary, it implements the bubble sort algorithm using nested for loops to iterate the array, compare and swap adjacent elements if out of order until the array is fully sorted.\",\"/src/Main.java\":\"This Java file contains a simple \\\"Hello World\\\" style program to demonstrate some basic IntelliJ IDEA features:\\n\\n- It defines a main method that prints a greeting string and then iterates from 1 to 5 printing the value of i each time. This provides a basic program flow to test and debug.\\n\\nKey functions:\\n\\n- main() - Standard Java entry point for applications\\n- System.out.print() - Prints a string to the console \\n- for loop - Iterates from 1 to 5\\n- System.out.println() - Prints a string and new line to the console\\n- Comments provide instructions on how to use IntelliJ features like:\\n - Showing whitespace characters\\n - Auto-fix suggestions\\n - Running the code \\n - Debugging with breakpoints\\n\\nSo in summary, it\\u0027s a basic \\\"Hello World\\\" style program that demonstrates running, debugging and some editor features of IntelliJ IDEA like auto-fix, whitespace visibility and breakpoints. The comments guide the user through interacting with these features.\",\"/src/calculation/star.java\":\"This Java file prints a star pattern using nested for loops:\\n\\n- It is in the calculation package\\n- It contains a main method, which is the entry point for the program\\n- It uses two for loops:\\n - The outer loop iterates from 0 to 4 (less than 5)\\n - The inner loop also iterates from 0 to 4\\n - On each iteration of the inner loop, it prints a \\\"*\\\"\\n- After the inner loop finishes, it prints a new line \\n- This continues for each iteration of the outer loop\\n- The end result is a 5x5 grid of stars printed out\\n\\nKey functions:\\n\\n- main() - Entry point for the program\\n- System.out.print() - Prints the \\\"*\\\" character \\n- System.out.println() - Prints a new line \\n- for loop - Allows iterating a certain number of times\\n- Nested for loops - Allows printing the pattern in a grid\\n\\nSo in summary, it uses nested for loops to print a 5x5 grid of stars through repetitive printing and new lines.\",\"/src/calculation/modulus.java\":\"This Java file calculates the modulus (remainder) of a division operation:\\n\\n- It is in the calculation package\\n- The modulus class contains the main method\\n- It prints the result of 5%2 to the console\\n- % is the modulus operator that returns the remainder of a division\\n- 5/2 equals 2 with a remainder of 1\\n- So 5%2 returns 1\\n\\nKey functions:\\n\\n- main() - The entry point for the Java application \\n- System.out.println() - Prints the result of the modulus operation to the console\\n- % - The modulus operator that calculates the remainder of a division\\n\\nSo in summary, this simple Java file defines a modulus class with a main method that uses the modulus operator (%) to calculate the remainder of 5/2 and prints the result to the console. It demonstrates a basic modulus (remainder) calculation in Java.\"}"}
